title: The $1,200 mistake that taught me why my home fiber connection wasn’t enough for my bot.
author: Turbulent_Sun_9285
selftext:
Hey everyone, just wanted to share a bit of a cautionary tale/experience from my last six months of shifting toward a fully automated setup.

I’ve been running a custom Python-based mean reversion bot on the side. Backtest results were solid, and for the first few weeks, everything was "fine." I was running the script off an old NUC I had tucked away in my home office. I figured, "Hey, I have gigabit fiber, why pay for extra infrastructure?"

Then, last Tuesday’s volatility hit.

I had a series of limit orders that should have triggered and closed out during a fast price spike. On my screen, the price hit my target. In my logs, the bot sent the execution command. But by the time the exchange received and processed it, the price had already slipped. Instead of a tidy profit, I got filled at a price that turned the trade into a $1,200 loss due to the delay.

I realized that my "fast" home internet was still routing through a dozen hops before hitting the exchange servers, and my local power flicker earlier that morning had actually knocked the bot offline for 20 minutes without me knowing.

I spent the weekend researching how to actually professionalize this. I realized that if I’m trusting an algorithm with thousands of dollars, I can't run it on a machine that shares bandwidth with my Netflix streaming or a smart fridge.

I eventually migrated the whole stack over to. The difference in execution latency was night and day, down from 80-100ms at home to sub-5ms because the server is actually co-located near the exchange hubs. Plus, not having to worry about my home ISP doing "scheduled maintenance" at 2:00 AM is a massive weight off my shoulders.

If you’re still running bots from a local PC or a generic cloud provider that isn't optimized for trading traffic, check your logs for slippage. You might be losing more money on bad fills than you’d ever spend on a dedicated setup.

Anyone else had a "local hosting" horror story, or did you guys jump straight to a VPS from day one?

comments.author: ddchbr
